Many financial frauds have roughly the same shape. Investors give you money=
 because you tell them you are doing something safe and sensible with it. I=
n fact, you are using the money for some combination of (1) paying your per=
sonal expenses and (2) making wild gambles. The gambles do not work out, th=
e money is gone, the investors notice, you go to prison.
What if the gambles=C2=A0do=C2=A0work out? Well! I think a lawyer would tel=
l you that, if you are lying to your investors and using some of their mone=
y on personal expenses and putting the rest into wild gambles, and the gamb=
les pay off, that is=C2=A0also fraud. It is arguably=C2=A0just as much=C2=
=A0fraud: What matters, as a legal and moral matter,=C2=A0is that you were =
doing wild dumb gambles and lying, not whether your gambling turned out to =
be lucky. Still, it does feel like there is a practical difference. If inve=
stors give you $100 and you do a series of dumb things with it and give the=
m back $150, they probably won=E2=80=99t=C2=A0complain.=C2=A0What you did w=
as illegal, but you don=E2=80=99t go to prison=C2=A0just=C2=A0because you d=
id something illegal. You go to prison because you did something illegal an=
d prosecutors bother to prosecute you, and if all your investors are happy =
they are less likely to bother.
It is not too hard to find successful startup founders who lied to investor=
s on their path to success, who =E2=80=9Cfaked it til they made it,=E2=80=
=9D and who made it and are now treated as geniuses. Other startup founders=
 also faked it til they made it,=C2=A0but did not make it, and faking it an=
d not making it is just fraud. To some extent, you flip a coin, and if=C2=
=A0 you win the coin flip you get rich and it was not, in retrospect, fraud=
; if you lose the coin flip you don=E2=80=99t get rich but you do get arres=
ted.
 =E2=80=9CPharma Bro=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Martin Shkreli is unusual=C2=A0because:
=09He became a public figure for doing a variety of very annoying but non-f=
raudulent things, so everyone was primed to dislike him.=09He was also, tho=
ugh more quietly,=C2=A0doing financial fraud: He ran some hedge funds in wh=
ich he was taking wild gambles with investor money, losing it and lying to =
the investors.=09He took one last gamble with his investors=E2=80=99 money,=
 and it worked out great: He made all his money back and then some,=C2=A0an=
d he paid all his investors back with profits.

=E2=80=9CAll my investors made money=E2=80=9D is not technically a=C2=A0leg=
al=C2=A0defense to fraud charges, but it is ordinarily=C2=A0practically=C2=
=A0sufficient. Shkreli created enough special circumstances that it wasn=E2=
=80=99t. He spent about six years in prison.
But he=E2=80=99s out now and apparently spending his time  watching Sam Ban=
kman-Fried=E2=80=99s criminal trial. Bankman-Fried is on trial for taking b=
illions of dollars from customers of his crypto exchange, FTX, telling them=
 that he was doing something safe and sensible with it, and really using it=
 for some combination of (1) lavish personal and promotional expenses and (=
2) wild gambles.=C2=A0The gambles did not work out, the money is gone, the =
investors noticed, and Bankman-Fried is in jail now and facing decades in p=
rison. The issues at trial will be (1) whether Bankman-Fried=C2=A0knew=C2=
=A0that the customer money was going to wild gambles and (2) whether, in th=
e context of crypto a few years ago, it was reasonable to chuck all the cus=
tomer money into wild gambles. I don=E2=80=99t think it is impossible for B=
ankman-Fried to win on those issues, but it will be pretty tough.
Here, though, is a stranger question: What if the gambles did=C2=A0work out=
, and the money is all there?  The Block reported yesterday:

Artificial intelligence firm Anthropic has reportedly raised fresh funds fr=
om Google and other investors that could potentially boost its valuation to=
 as much as $30 billion =E2=80=94 and that=E2=80=99s a =E2=80=9Cgood sign=
=E2=80=9D for FTX creditors given Sam Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s earlier inves=
tment, a crypto bankruptcy lawyer said. ...
In April 2022, Anthropic raised $580 million in its series B funding round =
led by Sam Bankman-Fried, founder of now-bankrupt FTX.
The latest new funds and the potential increase in company valuation could =
=E2=80=9Cmake it possible=E2=80=9D for FTX to log a =E2=80=9C100% recovery =
rate=E2=80=9D in assets as part of the failed crypto exchange=E2=80=99s ban=
kruptcy proceeding, Kunchou Tsai, managing partner of Taiwan-based Enlighte=
n Law Group, told The Block.
Tsai, who has helped over 100 clients file their FTX claims, said there sho=
uld be a lock-up period for Anthropic investors to dispose of their holding=
s for startup investments like this. =E2=80=9CIt remains uncertain when the=
 FTX bankruptcy restructuring team would be able to sell off its [stake in =
Anthropic],=E2=80=9D he added. =E2=80=9CThey might have to wait until Anthr=
opic once goes public unless there are any special occurrences.=E2=80=9D
FTX 2.0 Coalition, a group of FTX creditors, said in a post on X (formerly =
Twitter) that the potential Anthropic=E2=80=99s increased valuation could p=
ut FTX's stake at around $3 billion to $4.5 billion and that =E2=80=9CFTX c=
ustomers now stand to be made whole.=E2=80=9D

I would take that with a whole lot of salt =E2=80=94=C2=A0a $30 billion val=
uation in a fundraising round doesn=E2=80=99t mean that FTX could sell $4.5=
 billion worth at that price today, and anyway   last I checked the hole at=
 FTX was bigger than that =E2=80=94=C2=A0but, uh, maybe?=C2=A0
My assumption is that, even if FTX did manage to sell its Anthropic stake, =
tomorrow,=C2=A0for enough money to make all of its customers and creditors =
whole, that wouldn=E2=80=99t change very much about Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s=
 trial. =E2=80=9CWe lucked into enough money to pay everyone back=E2=80=9D =
is not a legal defense to fraud, the prosecutors have already bothered to b=
ring a case against him, and people are already primed to dislike him becau=
se of all the fraud and the losing of customer money. Still it wouldn=E2=80=
=99t=C2=A0hurt=C2=A0him.
  [1]
=C2=A0Everyone charged with fraud says that they planned to pay their custo=
mers back, but it is helpful if you actually can.
Also this is Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s whole thing. FTX, and his trading firm=
 Alameda Research, were in some loose sense a mechanism to funnel money fro=
m FTX customers into lottery tickets on weird visions of the future. The ma=
in weird vision of the future was something like =E2=80=9Ccrypto takes over=
 the financial system, and FTX becomes the most legally compliant and domin=
ant financial firm in crypto=E2=80=9D; much of   FTX/Alameda=E2=80=99s bala=
nce sheet =E2=80=94=C2=A0particularly its huge stash of FTX=E2=80=99s own F=
TT tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0was a straightforward, leveraged, risky bet on tha=
t outcome. That bet did not work out.
But Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s background was not in crypto but in the effecti=
ve altruism movement, and particularly in the corners of effective altruism=
 that worry a lot about existential risks of pandemics and rogue artificial=
 intelligence. So FTX/Alameda had other weird bets that had the flavor of =
=E2=80=9Cartificial general intelligence will become incredibly powerful an=
d making it non-evil will be the most important thing in the world,=E2=80=
=9D and FTX chucked $500 million into Anthropic as part of this bet, and ma=
ybe it will pay out big? The only way for FTX to make customers whole would=
 be for some=C2=A0improbable bet to pay off,
  [2]
 but FTX was in the business of making a lot of different improbable bets s=
o you never know. If you steal all your customers=E2=80=99 money to buy lot=
tery tickets, that=E2=80=99s probably bad, but maybe one of them will win.

      SBF Stuff: Kelly, etc.
   =20
 =20

We also   talked about these issues yesterday, through the lens of a weird =
series of bets that Bankman-Fried made as an intern at Jane Street Capital =
and that are recounted in Michael Lewis=E2=80=99s book Going Infinite. I wa=
nt to mention a few updates on that discussion.=C2=A0
First, in the original version of yesterday=E2=80=99s column, I transposed =
the names in my first description of the bet, writing =E2=80=9CIf any inter=
n loses more than $65 that day, Bankman-Fried will pay Asher that intern=E2=
=80=99s losses above $65; if none do, Asher will pay Bankman-Fried the diff=
erence between $65 and the maximum loss,=E2=80=9D where I meant =E2=80=9CIf=
 any intern loses more than $65 that day, Asher will pay Bankman-Fried that=
 intern=E2=80=99s losses above $65; if none do, Bankman-Fried will pay Ashe=
r the difference between $65 and the maximum loss.=E2=80=9D This is now cor=
rected on the web and the Bloomberg Terminal, but if you read Money Stuff b=
y email you got the wrong description of the bet, and that is annoying and =
I=E2=80=99m sorry.
Second, a reader  tweeted a better trade: =E2=80=9CSBF also either missed o=
r passed on a one-sided bet with greater +EV that would have inflicted grea=
ter loss on the counterparty on the first flip: pay two other interns $1 ea=
ch to coin flip each other for $100.=E2=80=9D Yes. This trade would cost As=
her $35 (one intern loses $100 on the coin flip, $35=C2=A0more than the $65=
 strike price on Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s bet with Asher
  [3]
) and would make Bankman-Fried $33=C2=A0($35 from Asher minus the $2 he pay=
s the interns). Crucially, Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s actual trade =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0pay an intern $1 to flip a coin with=C2=A0him=C2=A0for $98 =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0had a slightly lower $32 expected value, and was=C2=A0risky; he had a=
 50/50 chance of winning $130 or losing $66.=C2=A0This=C2=A0trade =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0pay two interns to flip each other =E2=80=94=C2=A0has a guaranteed pr=
ofit of $33 with no risk at all.=C2=A0Bankman-Fried went with the riskier t=
rade.=C2=A0
Third, a bunch of readers wrote in with defenses of Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s=
 understanding of the Kelly criterion. Basically Bankman-Fried has said tha=
t he would be willing to risk much more money on bets with small positive e=
dge than most traders would, =E2=80=9Cbecause ultimately my utility functio=
n isn=E2=80=99t really logarithmic. It=E2=80=99s closer to linear.=E2=80=9D=
 I wrote:=C2=A0
This misunderstands why trading firms use the Kelly criterion.=C2=A0Jane St=
reet=C2=A0does not go around taking any bet with a positive expected value.=
 The point of Kelly is not about utility curves; it=E2=80=99s not =E2=80=9C=
having $200 is less than twice as pleasant as having $100, so you should be=
 less willing to take big risks for big rewards.=E2=80=9D The point of Kell=
y is about maximizing your chances of surviving and obtaining long-run retu=
rns: It=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cif you bet 50% of your bankroll on 1%-edge bets,=
 you=E2=80=99ll be more likely to win each bet than lose it, but if you kee=
p doing that you will probably lose all your money eventually.=E2=80=9D Kel=
ly is about sizing your bets so you can keep playing the game and make the =
most money possible in the long run. Betting more can make you more money i=
n the short run, but if you keep doing it you will end in ruin.
But readers pointed out that this is unfair to Bankman-Fried. He=E2=80=99s =
correct. If your utility function is truly linear, you should bet more than=
 Kelly; you should be willing to risk more of your bankroll on risky but po=
sitive-expected-value bets.=C2=A0The result is that you will=C2=A0probably=
=C2=A0lose all your money, but if you end up winning money you will win a l=
ot more than if you were more conservative.
Still I am right in that this=C2=A0really is a way to court ruin. A trading=
 firm like Jane Street probably has more use for an additional dollar than =
you or I do, but still it does not have a truly linear utility function, in=
 the sense that an investing firm with a 99% chance of losing all its money=
 and a 1% chance of multiplying it by 120 is=C2=A0obviously a bad investing=
 firm. But if=C2=A0you do the math, and you search your heart, and you conc=
lude that that bet is good for you,=C2=A0then, go nuts I guess. You=E2=80=
=99ll probably end up ruined, but, you know, the math works? As one reader =
put it to me in an email: =E2=80=9CI think SBF's interpretation of linear u=
tility is correct, and people's criticisms of it basically boil down to say=
ing linear utility is kind of crazy (which it kind of is!).=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
And in fact it seems like Bankman-Fried, maybe more than anybody else I=E2=
=80=99ve ever heard of, really did think that way. In March 2022,  Tyler Co=
wen asked him:

COWEN: Should a Benthamite be risk-neutral with regard to social welfare?
BANKMAN-FRIED: Yes, that I feel very strongly about.
COWEN: Okay, but let=E2=80=99s say there=E2=80=99s a game: 51 percent, you =
double the Earth out somewhere else; 49 percent, it all disappears. Would y=
ou play that game? And would you keep on playing that, double or nothing?

And Bankman-Fried said yes! And Cowen went on:

COWEN: Then you keep on playing the game. So, what=E2=80=99s the chance we=
=E2=80=99re left with anything? Don=E2=80=99t I just  St. Petersburg parado=
x you into nonexistence?
BANKMAN-FRIED: Well, not necessarily. Maybe you St. Petersburg paradox into=
 an enormously valuable existence. That=E2=80=99s the other option.

That=E2=80=99s insane! You keep flipping a slightly weighted coin. Every ti=
me it comes up heads, the population of humans in the universe doubles. If =
it comes up tails once, every human in the universe is dead forever. How ma=
ny times would you flip? Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s answer was =E2=80=A6=C2=A0=
as many times as it takes to kill everyone? Okay! Great! Nice utilitarianis=
m you=E2=80=99ve got there.
I mean whatever, it=E2=80=99s a weird hypothetical. But a related question =
for Bankman-Fried, in March 2022, would have been: =E2=80=9CIf you have a 1=
% chance of making a trillion dollars and a 99% chance of going bankrupt an=
d spending the rest of your life in prison, would you take that bet?=E2=80=
=9D Let us assume that that bet has a positive expected value, that having =
a trillion dollars to spend on pandemic preparedness and AI alignment and p=
olitics and whatever adds more than 100 times as much utility to the world =
as sending you to prison forever subtracts. Would you do it?
Nobody asked Bankman-Fried that exact question in March 2022, as far as I k=
now, but it turns out that that was the game he was playing. He was almost =
certainly going to lose! He lost. But that=E2=80=99s life with a linear uti=
lity function.

   =20
      Private credit
   =20
 =20

My   main thesis about financial markets these days is:
=09Interest rates have gone up a lot recently, and=09It=E2=80=99s been so l=
ong since that last happened that everybody lost the capacity to imagine it=
, and most financial news for the next few years will have basically the fo=
rm =E2=80=9Cpeople structured their lives in ways that implicitly assumed r=
ates would never go up, and then rates went up and the stuff they did looks=
 dumb.=E2=80=9D It looked fine at the time, if you just forgot that rates c=
ould go up. Everyone did. Now they remember.

That schematic description does not quite capture the collapse of FTX, thou=
gh it=E2=80=99s not unrelated either. I think it more or less completely ca=
ptures this year=E2=80=99s=C2=A0US regional banking crisis. But there are l=
ots of other examples, large and small.
Here is a funny, relatively small example from   Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0K=
atharine Hidalgo and Silas Brown:

Private credit funds have been raking in bonanza profits lately as a result=
 of rocketing interest rates, but their investors are starting to question =
whether they really deserve so much of the windfall.
Firms in this booming $1.5 trillion market typically lend at a floating rat=
e, meaning fund managers get much higher yields from borrowers as base rate=
s soar. This in turn lets funds blast through so-called =E2=80=9Churdle rat=
es,=E2=80=9D the point where they can begin to collect profit =E2=80=94 or =
=E2=80=9Ccarry=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 on their returns.
Now, many investors, known as limited partners, are asking for more flexibi=
lity on how the carry is calculated because of the perceived unfairness of =
managers making fortunes just because a central bank hikes rates. =E2=80=A6
Private credit firms usually have a hurdle rate of between 5% and 7%. When =
a fund=E2=80=99s returns hit that threshold, it can start sharing in the pr=
ofit, on top of the 1%-2% management fees collected through a fund=E2=80=99=
s life. Managers often get about 10%-15% of the returns once they=E2=80=99v=
e passed the hurdle =E2=80=94 a significant dent for investors who get all =
the profit before that happens. =E2=80=A6
With investors demanding flexibility, one popular option is pegging hurdles=
 to central bank rates, meaning they rise or fall according to monetary pol=
icy.
=E2=80=9CReturns are really dependent on the base rate,=E2=80=9D says Flori=
an Hofer, director for private debt at Golding Capital Partners. =E2=80=9CI=
 think there=E2=80=99s a fair alignment of interest between the funds and t=
he LPs for this.=E2=80=9D Funds are =E2=80=9Cworried that if base rates go =
down again they=E2=80=99ll lose their carry, so I think a floating hurdle r=
ate is a fair compromise,=E2=80=9D he adds.
Christian Wiehenkamp, chief investment officer at Perpetual Investors, over=
sees a fund that invests in private credit funds and has used a hurdle rate=
 pegged to base rates since 2021. He says it=E2=80=99s right to reward fund=
s for performance rather than rate hikes: =E2=80=9CWe need to earn cash plu=
s something to show we=E2=80=99re generating alpha that you wouldn=E2=80=99=
t get elsewhere.=E2=80=9D

I think that if you asked any financially literate person an abstract quest=
ion like =E2=80=9Cwhat is the right hurdle rate for a floating-rate debt ma=
nager=E2=80=99s performance fees,=E2=80=9D they would not answer with a num=
ber; they would answer =E2=80=9Cwell probably it should be floating.=E2=80=
=9D But for like a decade floating interest rates were near zero and 7% was=
 such an unimaginably juicy rate that a debt investor who could achieve it =
would look like a wizard. And, conversely, a floating-rate debt manager who=
 asked for a hurdle rate like SOFR + 3%, when the secured overnight financi=
ng rate was 0.05%, would look greedy: =E2=80=9CYou want us to pay for perfo=
rmance when you are earning only 3.05%?=E2=80=9D And now SOFR is 5.32% and =
the fees are wrong.

      People are worried about Travis Scott liquidity
   =20
 =20

My understanding of how popular musicians=E2=80=99 concerts work is:
=09Tickets go on sale for normal-seeming prices.=09Five seconds later, all =
of the tickets have been bought by bots, brokers, resellers, etc., and the =
show is sold out.=09The resellers sell the tickets online at whatever price=
 the market will bear, which is generally many times the face value of the =
tickets.

This understanding is, however, pretty biased by how I consume information.=
 I do not buy a lot of concert tickets. When I am aware of concert ticket p=
ricing dynamics, it is because people on the internet are saying =E2=80=9Cj=
ust paid $20,000 for two Taylor Swift tickets and it was worth it!=E2=80=9D=
 For all I know there are like two artists like that,
  [4]
=C2=A0and everyone else just sells tickets for $60 directly to people who a=
ctually want to go to the shows. The ones with speculative frenzies are jus=
t the ones I read about.
I=E2=80=99m not alone, though. If=C2=A0your=C2=A0model of concert tickets i=
s =E2=80=9Cresellers buy them on Ticketmaster for $60 and resell them on St=
ubHub at $1,000,=E2=80=9D then you might want to get in on that. You might =
find a way to buy dozens, or thousands, of tickets for $60 each, and then r=
esell them all at $1,000. Everyone might do that. But maybe this model is w=
rong? Maybe the market-clearing rate for a lot of concert tickets is in fac=
t $60? Maybe it=E2=80=99s less? Maybe lots of concerts don=E2=80=99t sell o=
ut at $60? Or maybe they do, but only to speculative resellers, who then ha=
ve nobody to sell to. Maybe every concert is oversubscribed, but a lot of t=
he buyers are flippers?
At 404, Jason Koebler  writes about Travis Scott=E2=80=99s tour:

Check StubHub right now, though, and you can find thousands of tickets to =
=E2=80=9Csold out=E2=80=9D shows in many cities for between $10 and $20, fa=
r below the face value for his cheapest tickets at $61.50 before fees when =
they first went on sale. =C2=A0
In ticket reseller lingo, Scott=E2=80=99s tour is a =E2=80=9Cbloodbath,=E2=
=80=9D the result of overzealous brokers and noobs =E2=80=9Coverbuying=E2=
=80=9D tickets based on a miscalculation of the likely value of his tickets=
 on the secondary market. Many brokers now stand to lose a lot of money on =
Scott=E2=80=99s shows. At least part of this buying frenzy was fueled by a =
bet placed by PFS Buyers Club, a credit card maxing site I wrote about earl=
ier this week that has recently pivoted from buying rare coins to buying co=
ncert tickets. =C2=A0PFS told its members to buy as many tickets to Scott=
=E2=80=99s shows as possible, according to emails viewed by 404 Media. PFS =
itself stands to lose more than $1 million on Travis Scott alone when all i=
s said and done, it told members.
Members of the club were promised by PFS that they would be reimbursed for =
any tickets they bought and would additionally get a $25 per ticket commiss=
ion. Hundreds of members bought thousands of tickets, most of which are now=
 selling for far below face value. I spoke to people who are currently floa=
ting tens of thousands of dollars on their credit cards for Travis Scott ti=
ckets they have no idea what to do with. PFS has been telling angry members=
 that it could go out of business because of how much money it stands to lo=
se from the bet.=C2=A0

=E2=80=9CTickets they have no idea what to do with=E2=80=9D? They can just =
go to the concerts! And bring, like, dozens of friends.=C2=A0Just arenas fi=
lled with disgruntled credit card points maximizers who have never heard of=
 Travis Scott, really great energy for those shows.
Part of the problem here is that, after these people bought all these ticke=
ts, =E2=80=9CScott announced that he was going to be adding shows in specif=
ic cities, thereby doubling the number of available tickets in some markets=
.=E2=80=9D If you are a fan who bought tickets because you want to see Trav=
is Scott, that=E2=80=99s fine; you still get to see your show, and you are =
not harmed by the fact that more people get to see him. In a normal concert=
 ticket dynamic, this is good. But if you are a speculator hoping for a pri=
ce pop, you can=E2=80=99t be happy that Travis Scott is competing with you =
to sell tickets. In a purely speculative dynamic, it is bad.

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Tuesday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">SBF Stuff: Anthropic</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Many financial frauds have roughly the same sh=
ape. Investors give you money because you tell them you are doing something=
 safe and sensible with it. In fact, you are using the money for some combi=
nation of (1) paying your personal expenses and (2) making wild gambles. Th=
e gambles do not work out, the money is gone, the investors notice, you go =
to prison.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What if the gambles=C2=A0<em>do=C2=A0</em>work=
 out? Well! I think a lawyer would tell you that, if you are lying to your =
investors and using some of their money on personal expenses and putting th=
e rest into wild gambles, and the gambles pay off, that is=C2=A0<em>also fr=
aud</em>. It is arguably=C2=A0<em>just as much=C2=A0</em>fraud: What matter=
s, as a legal and moral matter,=C2=A0is that you were doing wild dumb gambl=
es and lying, not whether your gambling turned out to be lucky. Still, it d=
oes feel like there is a practical difference. If investors give you $100 a=
nd you do a series of dumb things with it and give them back $150, they pro=
bably won=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>complain.=C2=A0</em>What you did was <em>illeg=
al</em>, but you don=E2=80=99t go to prison=C2=A0<em>just=C2=A0</em>because=
 you did something illegal. You go to prison because you did something ille=
gal <em>and </em>prosecutors bother to prosecute you, and if all your inves=
tors are happy they are less likely to bother.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is not <em>too </em>hard to find successful=
 startup founders who lied to investors on their path to success, who =E2=
=80=9Cfaked it til they made it,=E2=80=9D and who made it and are now treat=
ed as geniuses. Other startup founders also faked it til they made it,=C2=
=A0but did not make it, and faking it and not making it is just fraud. To s=
ome extent, you flip a coin, and if=C2=A0 you win the coin flip you get ric=
h and it was not, in retrospect, fraud; if you lose the coin flip you don=
=E2=80=99t get rich but you do get arrested.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/32936710.258424/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2Fy=
dGljbGVzLzIwMTUtMTItMTcvbWFydGluLXNoa3JlbGktYWNjdXNlZC1vZi1iZWluZy1zdXJwcml=

=9CPharma Bro=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/32936710.258424/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2Fyd=
GljbGVzLzIwMTUtMTItMTcvbWFydGluLXNoa3JlbGktYWNjdXNlZC1vZi1iZWluZy1zdXJwcmlz=
aW5nbHktZ29vZC1hdC1mcmF1ZD9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMDA1MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXV=
tPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzEwMDUmdXRtX2NhbXBhaW=
duPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC4eff3b17" itemprop=3D"StoryLink"=
 itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Martin Shkreli=
 is unusual</a>=C2=A0because:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He became a public figure for doing a=
 variety of very annoying but non-fraudulent things, so everyone was primed=
 to dislike him.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He was <em>also</=
em>, though more quietly,=C2=A0doing financial fraud: He ran some hedge fun=
ds in which he was taking wild gambles with investor money, losing it and l=
ying to the investors.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He took one=
 last gamble with his investors=E2=80=99 money, and it worked out great: He=
 made all his money back and then some,=C2=A0and he paid all his investors =
back with profits.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CAll my investors made money=E2=80=9D =
is not technically a=C2=A0<em>legal=C2=A0</em>defense to fraud charges, but=
 it is ordinarily=C2=A0<em>practically=C2=A0</em>sufficient. Shkreli create=
d enough special circumstances that it wasn=E2=80=99t. He <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32936710.258424/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnB=
yLm9yZy8yMDIyLzA1LzE5LzExMDAwMTkwNjMvcGhhcm1hLWJyby1tYXJ0aW4tc2hrcmVsaS1iZW=
VuLXJlbGVhc2VkLWZyb20tcHJpc29u/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0d8027ca" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
spent about six years in prison</a>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But he=E2=80=99s out now and apparently spendi=
ng his time <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/329367=
10.258424/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS93YWdpZWVhY2Mvc3RhdHVzLzE3MDk3ODEyNjY3M=
DUzNjMxNjE/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd88a743e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsc=
ope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoratio=
n: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000;=
 border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> watching Sam Bankman=
-Fried=E2=80=99s criminal trial</a>. Bankman-Fried is on trial for taking b=
illions of dollars from customers of his crypto exchange, FTX, telling them=
 that he was doing something safe and sensible with it, and really using it=
 for some combination of (1) lavish personal and promotional expenses and (=
2) wild gambles.=C2=A0The gambles did not work out, the money is gone, the =
investors noticed, and Bankman-Fried is in jail now and facing decades in p=
rison. The issues at trial will be (1) whether Bankman-Fried=C2=A0<em>knew=
=C2=A0</em>that the customer money was going to wild gambles and (2) whethe=
r, in the context of crypto a few years ago, it was reasonable to chuck all=
 the customer money into wild gambles. I don=E2=80=99t think it is <em>impo=
ssible</em> for Bankman-Fried to win on those issues, but it will be pretty=
 tough.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here, though, is a stranger question: What if =
the gambles <em>did=C2=A0</em>work out, and the money <em>is </em>all there=
? <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32936710.258424/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlYmxvY2suY28vcG9zdC8yNTQ2NjcvYW50aHJvcGljcy1uZXctcmFpc2U=
tY291bGQtbWVhbi1mdWxsLXBheW91dC1mb3ItZnR4LWNyZWRpdG9ycy1sYXd5ZXI/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cBa12dcced" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> The Block reported yesterday</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Artificial intelligence firm Anthropic has rep=
ortedly raised fresh funds from Google and other investors that could poten=
tially boost its valuation to as much as $30 billion =E2=80=94 and that=E2=
=80=99s a =E2=80=9Cgood sign=E2=80=9D for FTX creditors given Sam Bankman-F=
ried=E2=80=99s earlier investment, a crypto bankruptcy lawyer said. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In April 2022, Anthropic raised $580 million i=
n its series B funding round led by Sam Bankman-Fried, founder of now-bankr=
upt FTX.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The latest new funds and the potential increas=
e in company valuation could =E2=80=9Cmake it possible=E2=80=9D for FTX to =
log a =E2=80=9C100% recovery rate=E2=80=9D in assets as part of the failed =
crypto exchange=E2=80=99s bankruptcy proceeding, Kunchou Tsai, managing par=
tner of Taiwan-based Enlighten Law Group, told The Block.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tsai, who has helped over 100 clients file the=
ir FTX claims, said there should be a lock-up period for Anthropic investor=
s to dispose of their holdings for startup investments like this. =E2=80=9C=
It remains uncertain when the FTX bankruptcy restructuring team would be ab=
le to sell off its [stake in Anthropic],=E2=80=9D he added. =E2=80=9CThey m=
ight have to wait until Anthropic once goes public unless there are any spe=
cial occurrences.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">FTX 2.0 Coalition, a group of FTX creditors, s=
aid in a post on X (formerly Twitter) that the potential Anthropic=E2=80=99=
s increased valuation could put FTX's stake at around $3 billion to $4.5 bi=
llion and that =E2=80=9CFTX customers now stand to be made whole.=E2=80=9D<=
/p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I would take that with a whole lot of salt =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0a $30 billion valuation in a fundraising round doesn=E2=80=99t =
mean that FTX could sell $4.5 billion worth at that price today, and anyway=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32936710.258424/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDktMTMveWll=
bGRzdHJlZXQtbWlzc2VkLWEtYm9hdD9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMDA1MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWV=
kaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzEwMDUmdXRtX2NhbX=
BhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB49a2c84e" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  last I c=
hecked</a> the hole at FTX was bigger than that =E2=80=94=C2=A0but, uh, may=
be?=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My assumption is that, even if FTX did manage =
to sell its Anthropic stake, tomorrow,=C2=A0for enough money to make all of=
 its customers and creditors whole, that wouldn=E2=80=99t change very much =
about Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s trial. =E2=80=9CWe lucked into enough money t=
o pay everyone back=E2=80=9D is not a legal defense to fraud, the prosecuto=
rs have already bothered to bring a case against him, and people are alread=
y primed to dislike him because of all the fraud and the losing of customer=
 money. Still it wouldn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>hurt=C2=A0</em>him.<a class=3D"f=
ootnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoratio=
n: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000;=
 border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !impor=
tant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Everyone charged with fraud says that they planned to pay their c=
ustomers back, but it is helpful if you actually can.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also this is Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s whole thi=
ng. FTX, and his trading firm Alameda Research, were in some loose sense a =
mechanism to funnel money from FTX customers into lottery tickets on weird =
visions of the future. The main weird vision of the future was something li=
ke =E2=80=9Ccrypto takes over the financial system, and FTX becomes the mos=
t legally compliant and dominant financial firm in crypto=E2=80=9D; much of=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32936710.258424/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTEtMTQvZnR4=
LXMtYmFsYW5jZS1zaGVldC13YXMtYmFkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDEwMDUyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9=
tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMTAwNSZ1dG1fY2=
FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8d35ef67" itemprop=3D"Sto=
ryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; =
text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-c=
olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  FTX/A=
lameda=E2=80=99s balance sheet</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0particularly its huge sta=
sh of FTX=E2=80=99s own FTT tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0was a straightforward, le=
veraged, risky bet on that outcome. That bet did not work out.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s background was not=
 in crypto but in the effective altruism movement, and particularly in the =
corners of effective altruism that worry a lot about existential risks of p=
andemics and rogue artificial intelligence. So FTX/Alameda had other weird =
bets that had the flavor of =E2=80=9Cartificial general intelligence will b=
ecome incredibly powerful and making it non-evil will be the most important=
 thing in the world,=E2=80=9D and FTX chucked $500 million into Anthropic a=
s part of this bet, and maybe it will pay out big? The only way for FTX to =
make customers whole would be for some=C2=A0improbable bet to pay off,<a cl=
ass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparen=
t !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a> but FTX was in the business of making a lot of different improbable be=
ts so you never know. If you steal all your customers=E2=80=99 money to buy=
 lottery tickets, that=E2=80=99s probably bad, but maybe one of them will w=
in.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Third, a bunch of readers wrote in with defens=
es of Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s understanding of the Kelly criterion. Basical=
ly Bankman-Fried has said that he would be willing to risk much more money =
on bets with small positive edge than most traders would, =E2=80=9Cbecause =
ultimately my utility function isn=E2=80=99t really logarithmic. It=E2=80=
=99s closer to linear.=E2=80=9D I wrote:=C2=A0</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This misunderstands why trading fi=
rms use the Kelly criterion.=C2=A0<em>Jane Street=C2=A0</em>does not go aro=
und taking any bet with a positive expected value. The point of Kelly is no=
t about utility curves; it=E2=80=99s not =E2=80=9Chaving $200 is less than =
twice as pleasant as having $100, so you should be less willing to take big=
 risks for big rewards.=E2=80=9D The point of Kelly is about maximizing you=
r chances of surviving and obtaining long-run returns: It=E2=80=99s =E2=80=
=9Cif you bet 50% of your bankroll on 1%-edge bets, you=E2=80=99ll be more =
likely to win each bet than lose it, but if you keep doing that you will pr=
obably lose all your money eventually.=E2=80=9D Kelly is about sizing your =
bets so you can keep playing the game and make the most money possible in t=
he long run. Betting more can make you more money in the short run, but if =
you keep doing it you will end in ruin.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But readers pointed out that this is unfair to=
 Bankman-Fried. He=E2=80=99s correct. If your utility function is truly lin=
ear, you should bet more than Kelly; you should be willing to risk more of =
your bankroll on risky but positive-expected-value bets.=C2=A0The result is=
 that you will=C2=A0<em>probably=C2=A0</em>lose all your money, but if you =
end up winning money you will win a lot more than if you were more conserva=
tive.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still I am right in that this=C2=A0really is a=
 way to court ruin. A trading firm like Jane Street probably has more use f=
or an additional dollar than you or I do, but still it does not have a trul=
y linear utility function, in the sense that an investing firm with a 99% c=
hance of losing all its money and a 1% chance of multiplying it by 120 is=
=C2=A0<em>obviously a bad investing firm</em>. But if=C2=A0you do the math,=
 and you search your heart, and you conclude that that bet is good for <em>=
you,=C2=A0</em>then, go nuts I guess. You=E2=80=99ll probably end up ruined=
, but, you know, the math works? As one reader put it to me in an email: =
=E2=80=9CI think SBF's interpretation of linear utility is correct, and peo=
ple's criticisms of it basically boil down to saying linear utility is kind=
 of crazy (which it kind of is!).=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And in fact it seems like Bankman-Fried, maybe=
 more than anybody else I=E2=80=99ve ever heard of, really did think that w=
ay. In March 2022, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/32936710.258424/aHR0cHM6Ly9jb252ZXJzYXRpb25zd2l0aHR5bGVyLmNvbS9lcGlzb2Rlcy=

oryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Tyler=
 Cowen asked him</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">COWEN: Should a Benthamite be risk-neutral wit=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">BANKMAN-FRIED: Yes, <em>that </em>I feel very =

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">COWEN: Okay, but let=E2=80=99s say there=E2=80=
=99s a game: 51 percent, you double the Earth out somewhere else; 49 percen=

</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And Bankman-Fried said yes! And Cowen went on:=
</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">COWEN: Then you keep on playing the game. So, =

 you into nonexistence?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">BANKMAN-FRIED: Well, not necessarily. Maybe yo=
u St. Petersburg paradox into an enormously valuable existence. That=E2=80=
=99s the other option.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That=E2=80=99s insane! You keep flipping a sli=
ghtly weighted coin. Every time it comes up heads, the population of humans=

verse is dead forever. How many times would you flip? Bankman-Fried=E2=80=
=99s answer was =E2=80=A6=C2=A0as many times as it takes to kill everyone? =
Okay! Great! Nice utilitarianism you=E2=80=99ve got there.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean whatever, it=E2=80=99s a weird hypothet=
ical. But a related question for Bankman-Fried, in March 2022, would have b=
een: =E2=80=9CIf you have a 1% chance of making a trillion dollars and a 99=
% chance of going bankrupt and spending the rest of your life in prison, wo=
uld you take that bet?=E2=80=9D Let us assume that that bet has a positive =
expected value, that having a trillion dollars to spend on pandemic prepare=

ld you do it?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Nobody asked Bankman-Fried that exact question=
 in March 2022, as far as I know, but it turns out that that was the game h=
e was playing. He was almost certainly going to lose! He lost. But that=E2=
=80=99s life with a linear utility function.</p>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Private credit funds have been raking in bonan=
za profits lately as a result of rocketing interest rates, but their invest=
ors are starting to question whether they really deserve so much of the win=
dfall.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Firms in this booming $1.5 trillion market typ=
ically lend at a floating rate, meaning fund managers get much higher yield=
s from borrowers as base rates soar. This in turn lets funds blast through =
so-called =E2=80=9Churdle rates,=E2=80=9D the point where they can begin to=
 collect profit =E2=80=94 or =E2=80=9Ccarry=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 on their ret=
urns.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, many investors, known as limited partners=
, are asking for more flexibility on how the carry is calculated because of=
 the perceived unfairness of managers making fortunes just because a centra=
l bank hikes rates. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Private credit firms usually have a hurdle rat=
e of between 5% and 7%. When a fund=E2=80=99s returns hit that threshold, i=
t can start sharing in the profit, on top of the 1%-2% management fees coll=
ected through a fund=E2=80=99s life. Managers often get about 10%-15% of th=
e returns once they=E2=80=99ve passed the hurdle =E2=80=94 a significant de=
nt for investors who get all the profit before that happens. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With investors demanding flexibility, one popu=
lar option is pegging hurdles to central bank rates, meaning they rise or f=
all according to monetary policy.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CReturns are really dependent on the b=
ase rate,=E2=80=9D says Florian Hofer, director for private debt at Golding=
 Capital Partners. =E2=80=9CI think there=E2=80=99s a fair alignment of int=
erest between the funds and the LPs for this.=E2=80=9D Funds are =E2=80=9Cw=
orried that if base rates go down again they=E2=80=99ll lose their carry, s=
o I think a floating hurdle rate is a fair compromise,=E2=80=9D he adds.</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Christian Wiehenkamp, chief investment officer=
 at Perpetual Investors, oversees a fund that invests in private credit fun=
ds and has used a hurdle rate pegged to base rates since 2021. He says it=
=E2=80=99s right to reward funds for performance rather than rate hikes: =
=E2=80=9CWe need to earn cash plus something to show we=E2=80=99re generati=
ng alpha that you wouldn=E2=80=99t get elsewhere.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think that if you asked any financially lite=
rate person an abstract question like =E2=80=9Cwhat is the right hurdle rat=
e for a floating-rate debt manager=E2=80=99s performance fees,=E2=80=9D the=
y would not answer with a <em>number</em>; they would answer =E2=80=9Cwell =
probably it should be floating.=E2=80=9D But for like a decade floating int=
erest rates were near zero and 7% was such an unimaginably juicy rate that =
a debt investor who could achieve it would look like a wizard. And, convers=
ely, a floating-rate debt manager who asked for a hurdle rate like SOFR + 3=
%, when the secured overnight financing rate was 0.05%, would look greedy: =
=E2=80=9CYou want us to pay for performance when you are earning only 3.05%=
?=E2=80=9D And now SOFR is 5.32% and the fees are wrong.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My understanding of how popular musicians=E2=
=80=99 concerts work is:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Tickets go on sale for normal-seeming=
 prices.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Five seconds later, all o=
f the tickets have been bought by bots, brokers, resellers, etc., and the s=
how is sold out.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The resellers sel=
l the tickets online at whatever price the market will bear, which is gener=
ally many times the face value of the tickets.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This understanding is, however, pretty biased =
by how I consume information. I do not buy a lot of concert tickets. When I=
 am aware of concert ticket pricing dynamics, it is because people on the i=
nternet are saying =E2=80=9Cjust paid $20,000 for two Taylor Swift tickets =
and it was worth it!=E2=80=9D For all I know there are like two artists lik=

0000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bo=
ttom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color=
: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0and everyone else just sells tickets for $60 directly to people w=
ho actually want to go to the shows. The ones with speculative frenzies are=
 just the ones I read about.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I=E2=80=99m not alone, though. If=C2=A0<em>you=
r=C2=A0</em>model of concert tickets is =E2=80=9Cresellers buy them on Tick=
etmaster for $60 and resell them on StubHub at $1,000,=E2=80=9D then you mi=
ght want to get in on that. You might find a way to buy dozens, or thousand=
s, of tickets for $60 each, and then resell them all at $1,000. Everyone mi=
ght do that. But maybe this model is wrong? Maybe the market-clearing rate =
for a lot of concert tickets is in fact $60? Maybe it=E2=80=99s less? Maybe=
 lots of concerts don=E2=80=99t sell out at $60? Or maybe they do, but only=
 to speculative resellers, who then have nobody to sell to. Maybe every con=
cert is oversubscribed, but a lot of the buyers are flippers?</p>

decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color:=
 #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> writes abou=
t Travis Scott=E2=80=99s tour</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Check StubHub right now, though, and you can f=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In ticket reseller lingo, Scott=E2=80=99s tour=
 is a =E2=80=9Cbloodbath,=E2=80=9D the result of overzealous brokers and no=
obs =E2=80=9Coverbuying=E2=80=9D tickets based on a miscalculation of the l=
ikely value of his tickets on the secondary market. Many brokers now stand =
to lose a lot of money on Scott=E2=80=99s shows. At least part of this buyi=
ng frenzy was fueled by a bet placed by PFS Buyers Club, a credit card maxi=
ng site I wrote about earlier this week that has recently pivoted from buyi=
ng rare coins to buying concert tickets. =C2=A0PFS told its members to buy =
as many tickets to Scott=E2=80=99s shows as possible, according to emails v=
iewed by 404 Media. PFS itself stands to lose more than $1 million on Travi=
s Scott alone when all is said and done, it told members.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Members of the club were promised by PFS that =
they would be reimbursed for any tickets they bought and would additionally=
 get a $25 per ticket commission. Hundreds of members bought thousands of t=
ickets, most of which are now selling for far below face value. I spoke to =
people who are currently floating tens of thousands of dollars on their cre=
dit cards for Travis Scott tickets they have no idea what to do with. PFS h=
as been telling angry members that it could go out of business because of h=
ow much money it stands to lose from the bet.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CTickets they have no idea what to do =
with=E2=80=9D? They can just go to the concerts! And bring, like, dozens of=
 friends.=C2=A0Just arenas filled with disgruntled credit card points maxim=
izers who have never heard of Travis Scott, really great energy for those s=
hows.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Part of the problem here is that, after these =
people bought all these tickets, =E2=80=9CScott announced that he was going=
 to be adding shows in specific cities, thereby doubling the number of avai=
lable tickets in some markets.=E2=80=9D If you are a fan who bought tickets=
 because you want to see Travis Scott, that=E2=80=99s fine; you still get t=
o see your show, and you are not harmed by the fact that more people get to=
 see him. In a normal concert ticket dynamic, this is good. But if you are =
a speculator hoping for a price pop, you can=E2=80=99t be happy that Travis=
 Scott is competing with you to sell tickets. In a purely speculative dynam=
ic, it is bad.</p>
